name: Tomato Juice
recipeIngredient: An average of 23 pounds of tomatoes is needed per canner load of 7 quarts,
or an average of 14 pounds per canner load of 9 pints. A bushel weighs 53 pounds and yields 15 to 18 quarts of juice, averaging 3-1/4 pounds per quart.
recipeInstructions:
1. Wash the tomatoes.
2. Remove stems and trim off any bruised or discolored portions.
3. If desired to prevent juice from separating, quickly cut about 1 pound of fruit into quarters and put directly into a saucepan.
4. Heat the mixture immediately to boiling while crushing.
5. Continue to slowly add and crush freshly cut tomato quarters to the boiling mixture.
6. Make sure the mixture boils constantly and vigorously while adding the remaining tomatoes.
7. Simmer for 5 minutes after all pieces have been added.
8. If not concerned about juice separation, simply slice or quarter tomatoes into a large saucepan, crush, heat, and simmer for 5 minutes before juicing.
9. Press both types of heated juice through a sieve or food mill to remove skins and seeds.
10. Add bottled lemon juice or citric acid to jars (See acidification instructions).
11. Heat the juice again to boiling.
12. Add 1 teaspoon of salt per quart, if desired, to the jars.
13. Fill jars with hot tomato juice, leaving 1/2-inch headspace.
14. Adjust lids and process according to the method used:
- Table 1: Boiling-water canner processing times
- Pints at altitudes up to 1,000 ft: 35 minutes; 1,001-3,000 ft: 40 minutes; 3,001-6,000 ft: 45 minutes; above 6,000 ft: 50 minutes.
- Quarts at altitudes up to 1,000 ft: 40 minutes; 1,001-3,000 ft: 45 minutes; 3,001-6,000 ft: 50 minutes; above 6,000 ft: 55 minutes.
- Table 2 and Table 3: Dial-gauge and weighted-gauge pressure canner processing times and gauge pressures at various altitudes.
